title: 11 1 Partial Differential Equations
body:
TOPIC 11: PARTIAL DIFFERENTIAL EQUATIONS I
        11.1 PDES AND D’ALEMBERT’S SOLUTION


The equation
                                      ∂ 2u       2
                                               2∂ u
                                           = c                                            (1)
                                      ∂t2       ∂x2
is called the one-dimensional wave equation.

It models the oscillations of a tightly stretched string.

The solution u(x, t) describes the displacement of the string at position x and time t.

The constant c is determined by the physical characteristics of the string.


The general solution to the one dimensional wave equation is

                                 u = φ(x + ct) + ψ(x − ct)                                (2)

where φ and ψ are arbitrary functions. (This is D’Alembert’s solution of the wave
equation)


If we have an initial displacement of

                                         u(x, 0) = f (x)

then the solution to the one dimensional wave equation is
                                         1
                             u(x, t) =     [f (x + ct) + f (x − ct)]
                                         2



If we have an initial displacement of

                                                                ∂u(x, 0)
               u(x, 0) = f (x) and an initial velocity of                = g(x)
                                                                  ∂t
then the solution to the one dimensional wave equation is

                                                        1 x+ct
                                                          Z
                            1
                  u(x, t) = [f (x + ct) + f (x − ct)] +         g(s)ds.
                            2                           2c x−ct



Basic concepts
An equation involving one or more partial derivatives of an (unknown) function of two or
more independent variables is called a partial differential equation.

   • The order of the highest derivative is called the order of the equation.

                                               1
    • A p.d.e (partial differential equation) is linear if it is linear in all terms involving
     u and its partial derivatives

   • A solution of a p.d.e. is a function of several variables which satisfies the equation.

Some examples

                         ∂ 2u       2
                                  2∂ u
            (1)               = c           one dimensional wave equation
                         ∂t2       ∂x2
                         ∂u     ∂ 2u
            (2)             = c2 2          one dimensional heat equation
                         ∂t     ∂x
                         ∂ 2u ∂ 2u
            (3)              +     =0         two dimensional Laplace equation
                         ∂x2 ∂y 2
                            ∂u 2 ∂u
            (4)         (      ) =
                            ∂t     ∂x



(1)-(3) are second order, linear. (4) is first order non-linear.

We may, depending on the problem, have boundary conditions (the solution has some
given value on the boundary of some domain) or initial conditions (where the value of
the solution will be given at some initial time, e.g. t = 0).

There are few general methods for solving p.d.e’s. Sometimes simple partial integration
will work and we look at some elementary techniques in this lecture. However the main
tool is a process called separation of variable which (together with the associated use of
Fourier series) will be examined in detail for the remainder of the course.


Example 1 Solve the partial differential equation

                                          ∂ 2u
                                               = cos(y).
                                         ∂x∂y




                                                   F u(x, t) = x sin(y) + φ(x) + ψ(y) F


We see from the above example that there is a lot of freedom (probably too much) in the
solution of a p.d.e. This is why boundary and initial conditions play such a central role.

                                               2
 Example 2 Verify that u(x, t) = e−t sin(3x) is a solution to the one dimensional heat
equation
                                  ∂u     1 ∂ 2u
                                       =
                                   ∂t    9 ∂x2




Example 3 Consider the one dimensional wave equation

                                       ∂ 2u      ∂ 2u
                                            = 25
                                       ∂t2       ∂x2
with initial displacment

                                                         ∂u(x, 0)
             u(x, 0) = 6ex and an initial velocity of             = 10 cos(x)
                                                           ∂t
Show that D’Alembert’s solution is

                   u(x, t) = 3ex+5t + 3ex−5t + sin(x + 5t) − sin(x − 5t).

Verify that both the p.d.e. and the initial conditions are satisfied.




                                             3
 4
 The full proof for D’Alembert’s solution (making extensive use of the chain rule) is in
your printed notes.

Unfortunately D’Alembert’s solution only works for the wave equation. Separation of
variables is a more general technique which will keep us busy for the remainder of the
course. We will apply separation of variables not only to the wave equation but also to a
host of other p.d.e’s.
